checker: Codex (GPT-5), in a primary-text review separate from the GPT-5.5 extraction run
method: Compared every converted guide entry absent from the 2026-07-03 audit with its primary full-text transcription and, where needed, the publisher or archival record.

Counted bibliographic, design, sample, dataset, quantitative-result, finding, and limitation statements as separate checkable claims; excluded editorial recommendations and cross-paper synthesis unless directly testable.

Checked a stratified extract sample for source identity, dataset and task counts, headline results, participants versus observations, judges, generation selection, inference budget, human baselines, contamination risk, and LLM-judge dependence.
Verified globally that every converted paper has an extract, every extract points to the current source hash, and no source was truncated.

guide_audit.scope: All 31 converted guide entries not present in papers/review/discrepancies.json

results.notes: Confirmed the first general-purpose generation-task framing, three languages plus GIF subtasks, participation and vote counts, low item-level agreement, stronger split-half system-ranking stability, Gemini 2.5 Flash baseline result, and absence of a human-written baseline.

Confirmed the ambiguity/distinctiveness model, human-rating studies, 435-sentence analysis, and the finding that distinctiveness predicts fine-grained pun funniness. The catalog uses the 2016 journal issue year; the supplied version was first published online in 2015.
Confirmed the local-global surprisal proposal, SemEval/KAO analyses, SURGEN design, and generation success rates of 31.4%, 9.2%, and 78.9% for SURGEN, the neural baseline, and humans.
Confirmed the 15,199-article context corpus, retrieve-edit-rerank pipeline, two accepted generated headlines, and the reported percentile result for the stronger accepted headline.
Confirmed the location-specific pun-generation framing and the BERT ranking plus Mad-Libs-style generation pipeline.
Confirmed the controlled 1,000-item cross-modal pun resource, its three tasks, and the persistent model-human gap.

Confirmed the five-model Qwen3 comparison, perfectly monotonic automated size ranking, position/length effects, 12 blinded human annotators, nonsignificant aggregate difference among the three largest models, and the post-hoc p=.039, Kendall's W=.025 result.
Confirmed the three professional-comedian raters, 240 jointly rated jokes, one-million-Reddit-joke reference corpus, and Spearman correlations of .37 and .40.
Confirmed the 120,000-word/phrase collection stage, embedding-based preference representation, individual ratings, theory-motivated features, and demographic preference clustering.

discrepancies.guide_said: The current Jester releases contain 6.5M ratings from roughly 150K users.
discrepancies.source_says: The official dataset page states 6.5M anonymous ratings and gives release-specific user counts, but does not state a deduplicated combined user total.
discrepancies.resolution: Removed the approximate combined user count and described the 6.5M ratings as spanning successive releases over 100–158 jokes.

results.notes: Confirmed Eigentaste's constant-time collaborative-filtering contribution against the supplied manuscript and current release statistics against the official Jester dataset page. The catalog distinguishes the manuscript's 2000 date from the 2001 journal issue.
Confirmed the roughly 40-hour stand-up corpus, 0–4 humor-quotient scale, and approximately .60 quadratic-weighted-kappa agreement.

Confirmed the 5,416-item corpus, 90 series, 24 genre labels, negative/positive correlations around -.75/.28, and the qualification that the Netflix material is edited television rather than live interaction.

Confirmed five expert interviews, 58 analyzed videos, 32 study participants, the 4.89 versus 3.72 humor ratings with corrected p=.03, and the identity/scaffolding confound that prevents a pure model-capability interpretation.
Confirmed the preregistered 600-image audit, human coding/pretests, and the trait-specific stereotype shifts after the instruction to make images funnier.

Confirmed the counterfactual speaker/listener design, maximum reported asymmetries of 67.5%, 64.7%, and 1.5 percentage points across tasks, and the use of human and Llama validation for automated judgments.

Confirmed 4,379 memes, annotation design, three prediction tasks, and multimodal benchmark results. The paper's conclusion contains an internal 4,397 typo; the guide correctly follows the abstract and split totals.
Confirmed the Hindi long-context resource size, video/segment structure, model comparisons, context-length effects, and limited human validation of generated ground truth.
Confirmed the 1,405 English puns, 5,838 French translations, 376 new sources with 832 references, task/run counts, automatic rankings, and low prior manually evaluated wordplay-preservation rates.

Confirmed the six-model HSQ design, 1,000 simulated response sets per model, 1,071-response human reference, low human-model factor-correlation similarity, and failure to recover the human four-factor structure.
Confirmed 348 comics, annotation fields, four tasks, human verification, and the reported literal-perception and cross-panel reasoning failures.
Confirmed the separate 1,262-comic multilingual YesBut V2 lineage, its tasks, and reported perception, comparison, and hallucination gaps.
Confirmed the source-blind human discrimination design and its interpretation as authorship indistinguishability rather than direct proof of superior funniness.

discrepancies.guide_said: The paper's checklist calls for population, recruitment source, language/culture, expertise, and aggregation protocol.
discrepancies.source_says: Its proposed evaluation statement calls for evaluator number and sample assignment, recruitment and compensation, demographic information, task proficiency, definitions, pilot-driven amendments, training materials, and inter-annotator agreement; it does not explicitly name an aggregation protocol.
discrepancies.resolution: Replaced the paraphrase with the paper's actual reporting categories.

results.notes: Confirmed that this is a position paper with a critical survey of 17 generation papers, not an effect-size meta-analysis, and confirmed its underreporting findings.

Confirmed three professional improvisers, 54 jokes, 40 audience members, 2,160 ratings, 34.6%/29.7%/35.7% pairwise preferences, 2.67/2.59 means, and offstage human selection from multiple GPT-4 candidates.

discrepancies.guide_said: All 890 non-pun distractors were minimally substituted.
discrepancies.source_says: The negatives use both explicative substitution and random substitution; the latter can also regenerate the image.
discrepancies.resolution: Changed the characterization to 'substitution-based non-pun distractors.'

results.notes: Confirmed 445 positive puns, 890 negatives, three tasks, affirmative pun-pattern failures, and the average 16.5% F1 improvement from Pun-CoT/Pun-Tuning.
Confirmed the four-study design, task-dependent comparisons, human and GPT-4o evaluator roles, and the limits on generalizing across generation, coping, evaluation, and workplace-conflict settings.
Confirmed the 200-tweet humor-presence sample, 90 humorous tweets for function labels, exact agreement values, and reliance on one expert reference annotator.

discrepancies.guide_said: The author order followed ACL metadata: Jansen, Truong, Heylen & Nazareth.
discrepancies.source_says: The proceedings PDF lists Jansen, Truong, Nazareth & Heylen.
discrepancies.resolution: Synchronized the guide and canonical paper catalog to the author order printed on the primary paper.

results.notes: Confirmed 2h14 of annotated interaction, 601 laughs, 168 speech-laughs, 538 respiration events, participant self/partner perceptions, personality/physiology measures, acoustic findings, and individual-license access.

discrepancies.guide_said: Five interactions had received the full enrichment pipeline, implying smile and humor annotations.
discrepancies.source_says: Five interactions had speech/transcription enrichment, but only two had manual smile and humor annotations and entered the exploratory analysis.
discrepancies.resolution: Made the five-versus-two distinction explicit in both the guide and dataset catalog.

results.notes: Confirmed 11 roughly 15-minute conversations, 22 French-speaking friends, the smile/humor/reaction annotation scheme, exploratory success/failure analysis, and mixed-access distribution.

discrepancies.guide_said: The compact citation treated Korean given names from ACL metadata as surnames.
discrepancies.source_says: The PDF displays Lee Jung-Mok, Kim Sung-Bin, Joohyun Chang, Lee Hyun, and Tae-Hyun Oh.
discrepancies.resolution: Changed the compact guide citation to the unambiguous 'Lee et al.' while retaining the full display names in the catalog.

results.notes: Confirmed the detection/type/reasoning tasks, 3,590 clips and 6,386 QA pairs, human verification, self-instruction, Mixture-of-Laugh-Experts, public-video sourcing, and textualized multimodal inputs.

Confirmed 250 synthetic contexts by 400 manga panels, 100,000 pairs, five labels per pair, 500,000 annotations, 2,325 crowdworkers, Fleiss' kappa of -.022, consensus subsets, model results, and lack of visual-input benefit. The paper's release URL was still a placeholder when checked, so the catalog correctly marks the benchmark unavailable.

extract_audit.selection: Purposeful stratified sample spanning theory, Parts 1–9, the adjacent section, 2010–2026, datasets/benchmarks/HCI/surveys, text and multimodal work, human-only and LLM-heavy designs, single-sample and best-of-N generation, all four newly added conversational resources, and none/secondary/substantial LLM-judge dependence.

results.notes: Human-only experimental design, five-study samples/results, observer role, and not-applicable model fields match the source.
Stimulus/rater counts, fitted measures, headline statistics, and absence of LLM judging match; the extract correctly uses the online-version year.
704 cartoons versus 2.7K captions, task results, human estimates, beam selection, construction filters, and contamination warning match.
Dataset splits, model results, five-person error analysis, single-output evaluation, and no LLM-as-judge dependence match.
The profile correctly separates 32/21/17 respondents from 6,015/4,765/4,044 ratings and records best-of-30 generation followed by GPT-4o filtering.
Fifty rounds, 250 matched pairs, five raters, primary human results, single-output conditions, and secondary GPT-5.1 diagnostic use match.
Stimulus and worker counts, correlation and success results, retrieval candidate budget, and directly comparable expert-written baseline match.
Dataset counts, validation/results, human baselines, and unresolved 650-versus-850 participant-reporting conflict are faithfully preserved.
Dataset/tasks, eight annotators, three-person evaluation subset, human verification, and secondary GPT construction/scoring dependence match.
Positive/negative counts, three-annotator acceptance, generation path, no human performance baseline, and secondary LLM explanation judge match.
Single joke per model/theme, 12 human annotators versus 240 rankings, automated-judge biases, and substantial judge dependence match.
Collection stages, 1,678-person study, embedding results, static-model design, and no LLM judging match.
Non-empirical profile, survey flow/counts, reporting results, and not-applicable generation/baseline fields match.
Interview/video/study samples, matched-condition results, unreported output-selection budget, lack of human-performance baseline, and human evaluation match.
Three performers, 40 audience members versus 2,160 ratings, live matched human baseline, and offstage best-of-multiple AI selection match.
The profile distinguishes 32 recruited/6 excluded/26 included, 357 recorded versus 134 annotated minutes, event counts, participant ratings, and no model evaluation.
Eleven dyads, five speech-enriched versus two manually smile/humor-annotated interactions, coder roles, kappa values, and descriptive limitations match.
Clip/QA/split counts, majority-vote type labels, unreported annotator population, GPT-4 label/synthetic-data role, and no directly comparable human baseline match.
The profile keeps 2,325 annotators distinct from 500,000 annotations and correctly records fixed-candidate selection, human comparison, and no LLM judge.
Two research-assistant accounts/coders, participant pretests, paired single-sample generations, human labeling, and no human creative baseline match.
Task/item counts, human validators, single-output generation, automated judgments with validation, and substantial LLM-judge dependence match.
Benchmark/result counts, explicitly unreported annotator N, generated-reference risk, no human baseline, and automatic-metric evaluation match.
Parallel/reference counts, system rankings, translator/annotation roles, missing budgets, and conservative learned-language-model-metric dependence match the source and evidence schema.
Users, teams, submissions, paid/volunteer votes, skips, system-level agreement, heterogeneous participant candidate budgets, and missing human-written baseline are kept distinct and match.
Survey counts, conversational-humor gap, and explicit non-empirical/no-judge profile match.
The profile distinguishes 1,071 archival human respondents from 1,000 model response sets per model and correctly records matched psychometric rather than human-generation baselines.

limitations: This was an AI-performed second review, not an independent human replication of the extraction pipeline.
The 26 extracts received substantive spot checks; the other 85 received source-presence, hash, and truncation checks only.
A passed spot check is evidence against obvious extraction error in the checked fields, not certification of every sentence or analyst judgment in that extract.
